title: Floating Point Packing Codes (F)
text: Floating Point Packing Codes (F) When high precision is required, use the F packing code, which stores numbers as an exponent (2 n ) and a mantissa (the number by which to multiply the exponent). Note: If no packing code is specified for a field with data type Float, Uniface defaults to a packing code of C20 rather than F . Default Syntax Length Table 1. Default Syntax Length for Floating Point Packing Codes Data type Packing code Default syntax length Numeric F, F4, F8 20 Float F F4 F8 20 12 22 If you have problems with floating point, always consult the connector documentation for your DBMS. Range of Values F4 and F8 offer the same range of number size, because the space used for the exponent section is the same for both F4 and F8: F4 provides an accuracy of 7 digits for the mantissa. F8 provides an accuracy of 14 digits for the mantissa. The F4 and F8 packing codes are often described as single- and double-precision, respectively. Be aware that the accuracy explained here is machine- and DBMS-dependent. Table 2. Range of Values for F Packing Codes Packing code Range of exponent Accuracy of mantissa (DBMS-dependent) Default syntax length F F4 +/- (10 +/-38 ) 7 digits 12 F8 +/- (10 +/-38 ) 14 digits 22 For example, the maximum accuracy of the mantissa section of an F4 field could be 1.234567 (which is then multiplied by the exponent) and for an F8 field 1.23456789012345. These figures are DBMS-dependent; for more information, see your DBMS documentation. Uniface guarantees an accuracy of six digits for F4, and 14 digits for F8, as specified in the ANSI/IEEE 75-1985 standard. Scaling The syntax for scaling with the F packing code is: F m { . n } m is the total length of the field to be stored in bytes n is the (optional) number of digits after the decimal. If n is present, a period ( . ) must separate n from m . Scaling of floating point fields is something of a contradiction, because a floating point field uses scaling by definition. Defining scaling with a floating point field determines the default display format, but is of no other use. If scaling is defined for the packing code, or a display format is defined for the field, Uniface applies the appropriate restrictions for both data entry and the display of retrieved data. This prevents the use of scientific notation for data entry and display. Scientific Notation Floating point fields defined as data type Float or Numeric can accept a scientific notation. For example, you could enter 1.23e-5 in a field with data type Float and 123e-5 in a field with data type Numeric. Uniface formats and converts data once it is entered. Scientific notation does not affect the working of ProcScript; for example, the following line of ProcScript causes no problems if any of the referenced fields contain data that has been entered in scientific notation: FIELD1 = FIELD2 + FIELD3 Copy All arithmetic functions are possible with scientific notation. This includes combinations of Numeric and Float data types. However, if scaling is defined for the packing code, scientific notation cannot be used for data entry and display. Related Links Packing Codes for Numeric Data The Uniface Numeric and Float data types are associated with many packing codes. Database Connectors To access, retrieve, and store data, Uniface provides database connectors that translate Uniface I/O requests into the target database's data definition and manipulation languages.
